evidence: The Océan class ironclads were a group of three wooden-hulled armored frigates built for the French Navy in the mid to late 1860s.
Suffren was often used as the flagship for the squadron she was assigned to.
The ships were central battery ironclads with the armament concentrated amidships.
The ships measured 87.73 meters overall, with a beam of 17.52 meters.
These ships had their main armament mounted in four barbettes on the upper deck.
The original armament consisted of four 240-millimeter guns in the barbettes.
Marengo was a wooden-hulled, armored frigate of the Océan class, built for the French Navy in the mid to late 1860s.
She was sold for scrap in 1896.
The Océan-class ironclads were designed by Henri Dupuy de Lôme as an improved version of the Provence-class ironclads.
Marengo had a maximum draft of 9.09 meters and displaced 7,749 metric tons.
Marengo's armament was upgraded to four 274-millimeter guns in the barbettes.
Océan was a wooden-hulled, armored frigate built for the French Navy in the mid to late 1860s and the lead ship of her class.
The ship later served in both the Northern and Mediterranean Squadrons.
The Océan-class ironclads were designed by Henri Dupuy de Lôme as an improved version of the Provence-class ironclads.
Océan had a maximum draft of 9.09 meters and displaced 7,749 metric tons.
Océan's original armament consisted of four 240-millimeter guns in the barbettes.
Tifon was an Uragan-class monitor built for the Imperial Russian Navy in the mid-1860s.
The ship was abandoned by the Soviets in Finland in 1918.
Tifon was 201 feet long overall, with a beam of 46 feet and a draft of 10.16 – 10.84 feet.
The ship was fitted with a two-cylinder, horizontal direct-acting steam engine.
Tifon was designed to be armed with a pair of 9-inch smoothbore muzzle-loading guns.
The French ironclad Thétis was a wooden-hulled armored corvette built for the French Navy in the late 1860s.
Thétis was eventually hulked in New Caledonia.
Thétis measured 69.03 meters between perpendiculars, with a beam of 14.13 meters.
She had a mean draft of 6.26 meters and displaced 3,569 metric tons.
Thétis mounted her four 194-millimeter Modèle 1864 breech-loading guns in the central battery.
HMS Boreas was a B-class destroyer built for the Royal Navy around 1930.
She was loaned to the Royal Hellenic Navy the next year after conversion into an escort destroyer.
Boreas displaced 1,360 long tons at standard load and 1,790 long tons at deep load.
The ship had an overall length of 323 feet.
The ship mounted four 45-calibre quick-firing 4.7-inch Mk IX guns.
HMS Hostile was an H-class destroyer built for the Royal Navy in the 1930s.
She was transferred to Freetown, Sierra Leone, in October 1939.
Hostile displaced 1,350 long tons at standard load and 1,883 long tons at deep load.
The ship had an overall length of 323 feet.
The ship mounted four 45-calibre 4.7-inch Mark IX guns in single mounts.
The French ironclad Friedland was originally intended to be an iron-hulled version of Océan class armoured frigate.
She spent the bulk of her career assigned to the Mediterranean Squadron.
Friedland was a central battery ironclad with the armament concentrated amidships.
The ship measured 101.1 meters overall, with a beam of 17.7 meters.
Two 274-millimetre Modèle 1870 guns were mounted in barbettes on the upper deck.
The German submarine U-41 was a Type IXA U-boat of Nazi Germany's Kriegsmarine that operated during World War II.
U-41 also sank five enemy vessels for a total of 22,815 gross register tons.
U-41 was ordered by the Kriegsmarine on 21 November 1936.
She was launched on 28 January 1939 and commissioned on 22 April of that same year.
U-41 had a displacement of 1,032 tonnes when at the surface and 1,153 tonnes while submerged.
The submarine was powered by two MAN M 9 V 40 / 46 supercharged four-stroke engines.
The French ironclad Reine Blanche was a wooden-hulled armored corvette built for the French Navy in the late 1860s.
The ship bombarded the port of Sfax during the French occupation of Tunisia in 1881.
Reine Blanche measured 69.02 meters between perpendiculars, with a beam of 13.94 meters.
She had a mean draft of 6.48 meters and displaced 3,768 metric tons.
Reine Blanche mounted her four 194-millimeter Modèle 1864 breech-loading guns in the central battery.

evidence: The 20th Battalion was formed in New Zealand in 1939 under the command of Lieutenant Colonel Howard Kippenberger.
It participated in the Battles of Greece and later in Crete.
It suffered heavy losses during Operation Crusader, when it was effectively destroyed by the 15th Panzer Division.
The battalion played a key role in the breakout of the 2nd New Zealand Division from Minqar Qaim in June 1942.
The 2nd Parachute Brigade was an airborne forces brigade formed by the British Army during the Second World War.
It was initially part of the 1st Airborne Division but in 1943 became an independent formation.
The brigade saw active service in Italy, the South of France and Greece.
Before the end of the European war, the brigade returned to the United Kingdom.
The 26th Battalion was an infantry battalion of the Australian Army.
It was assigned to the 7th Brigade and consisted of personnel recruited from Queensland and Tasmania.
The battalion was used in various garrison roles in the early part of World War II before a detachment was sent to the Dutch East Indies.
The 23rd Battalion was raised in Victoria in March 1915 as part of the formation of the 2nd Division of the Australian Imperial Force.
It was sent to Egypt to complete its training before being committed to the Gallipoli Campaign.
They remained on the peninsula until the evacuation of Allied troops in December.
The decision was made by Allied commanders to move the 2nd Division from Egypt.
The 2/6th Battalion was an infantry battalion of the Australian Army that served during the Second World War.
It took part in the first action of the war by Australian ground forces, the Battle of Bardia.
The battalion was withdrawn from the Middle East to help face the threat posed by the Japanese in the Pacific.
They were committed to the Aitape-Wewak campaign in late 1944.
The 25th Battalion was an infantry battalion of the New Zealand Military Forces, which served during the Second World War.
It was formed in New Zealand in 1940 and after a period of training, it embarked for the Middle East.
The battalion participated in the Italian Campaign, fighting in actions at Orsogna and later at Cassino.
It finished the war in Trieste and remained there to counter the presence of Yugoslav partisans.
The 2/31st Battalion was an infantry battalion of the Australian Army that served during World War II.
It was raised in June 1940 and was assigned to the 25th Brigade, 7th Division.
The battalion was sent to New Guinea to fight against the Japanese along the Kokoda Track.
They subsequently fought a number of battles throughout 1942, 1943 and early 1944.
The 2/15th Battalion was an infantry battalion of the Australian Army that served during World War II.
Formed in May 1940 primarily from Queensland volunteers, the battalion saw action in North Africa.
The battalion returned to North Africa to fight in the First and Second Battles of El Alamein.
It took part in campaigns against the Japanese in New Guinea in 1943 – 44.
The 15th Battalion was formed in 1914 as part of the all-volunteer Australian Imperial Force from Queensland and Tasmanian recruits.
During the Second World War the 15th Battalion was mobilised for wartime service.
The battalion was mobilised for wartime service and initially undertook defensive duties in Australia before taking part in the fighting against the Japanese in New Guinea.
The 14th/32nd Battalion was formed in September 1942 by the amalgamation of the 14th and 32nd Battalions.
The battalion served firstly in Australia and then later New Guinea.
The battalion was moved to the Atherton Tablelands in Queensland to undertake amphibious landing and jungle warfare training.
They were sent to Port Moresby, New Guinea, where they once again filled the role of garrison troops.

evidence: The Australian weasel shark (Hemigaleus australiensis) is an uncommon species of ground shark in the family Hemigaleidae.
Feeding almost exclusively on octopuses and other cephalopods, the Australian weasel shark mostly hunts near the sea floor.
It is viviparous, with the developing embryos nourished through a placental connection and born after a six-month gestation period.
Females bearing litters of 1 – 19 pups usually twice per year.
The sicklefin weasel shark (Hemigaleus microstoma) is an uncommon species of ground shark in the family Hemigaleidae.
Spending most of its time close to the sea floor, the sicklefin weasel shark is a specialist predator of cephalopods.
Its reproductive mode is viviparous, in which the unborn young form a placental connection to their mother.
Females probably give birth twice a year, with each litter consisting of two to four pups.
The banded houndshark (Triakis scyllium) is a species of houndshark, in the family Triakidae, common in the northwestern Pacific Ocean.
This shark reaches 1.5 m (4.9 ft) in length.
It is aplacental viviparous, with the developing embryos sustained by yolk.
Females bear as many as 42 pups following a gestation period of 9 – 12 months.
The Izak catshark (Holohalaelurus regani) is a species of catshark, belonging to the family Scyliorhinidae.
This species reaches 69 cm (27 in) in length.
Bottom-dwelling and perhaps relatively active in nature, the Izak catshark feeds mainly on a diverse array of bony fishes, crustaceans, and cephalopods.
The hardnose shark (Carcharhinus macloti) is a species of requiem shark, in the family Carcharhinidae.
A small bronze-coloured shark reaching a length of 1.1 m (3.6 ft).
This species is viviparous, with the growing embryos sustained to term via a placental connection to their mother.
Females have a biennial reproductive cycle and bear litters of one or two pups after a twelve-month gestation period.
The thornback guitarfish (Platyrhinoidis triseriata) is a species of ray in the family Platyrhinidae.
Reaching 91 cm (36 in) in length, the thornback guitarfish has a heart-shaped pectoral fin disc.
It is aplacental viviparous, with the developing young drawing sustenance from a yolk sac.
Females give birth to 1 – 15 pups annually in late summer, following a roughly year-long gestation period.
The bigeye sand tiger (Odontaspis noronhai) is an extremely rare species of mackerel shark in the family Odontaspididae.
A large, bulky species reaching at least 3.6 m (12 ft) in length.
It feeds on bony fishes and squid.
The mangrove whipray or whitetail stingray (Himantura granulata) is a species of stingray in the family Dasyatidae.
It grows up to 1.4 m (4.6 ft) across.
It is, like other stingrays, aplacental viviparous, with the females nourishing their unborn young via histotroph ("uterine milk").
The cloudy catshark (Scyliorhinus torazame) is a common species of catshark, belonging to the family Scyliorhinidae.
Growing up to 50 cm (20 in) long, this small, slim shark has a narrow head.
It is oviparous, with females laying encapsulated eggs two at a time in nursery areas.
The sparsely-spotted stingaree, white-spotted stingaree, or Dixon's stingaree (Urolophus paucimaculatus) is a species of stingray in the family Urolophidae.
Reaching a length of 57 cm (22 in), this species has a broad, diamond-shaped pectoral fin disc.
It is aplacental viviparous, with the mother provisioning her young with histotroph ("uterine milk").

The game was released in Japan on April 29, 2009.
The game is a turn-based RPG that allows players to take on the role of six characters from the series.
The battle screen will take place at the screen with the party facing off with a monster or a boss.
Super Mario World is a 1990 platform video game developed and published by Nintendo as a pack-in launch title for the Super Nintendo Entertainment System (SNES).
It built on the gameplay of previous Mario games, introducing new power-ups that augment character abilities.
The game shares similar gameplay mechanics with previous titles in the series.
The player navigates through the game via two game screens: an overworld map and a sidescrolling playfield.
Naruto : Clash of Ninja is a 3-D cel-shaded fighting game developed by Eighting and published by D3 Publisher and Tomy.
It is based on the popular anime and manga series Naruto by Masashi Kishimoto.
Players pit two characters from the Naruto manga and anime series against each other.
The objective is to reduce the opponent's health to zero using basic attacks and special techniques.
Mortal Kombat : Deception is a fighting game developed and published by Midway as the sixth installment of the Mortal Kombat series.
Its story centers on the revival of the Dragon King Onaga.
The game also introduces the 'Combo Breaker', a system which allows players to interrupt combos up to three times per match.
The game introduces two minigames that use MK characters.
Marvel vs. Capcom 2 is a crossover fighting game developed and published by Capcom.
Players select a team of characters from the Marvel and Capcom universes to engage in combat.

X-Men Legends II is an action role-playing game developed primarily by Raven Software and published by Activision.

evidence: The Kaivalya Upanishad is an ancient Sanskrit text and one of the minor Upanishads of Hinduism.
The text is notable for presenting Shaivism in Vedanta, discussing Atman (Soul, Self) and its relation to Brahman.
The Upanishad extols Shiva, aloneness and renunciation, describes the inner state of man in his personal spiritual journey detached from the world.
The Gospel According to John is one of the four canonical gospels in the New Testament.
John begins with the witness and affirmation of John the Baptist and concludes with the death, burial, resurrection, and post-resurrection appearances of Jesus.
The community appears to define itself primarily in contrast to Judaism, rather than as part of a wider Christian community.
The Tithonus poem is a poem by the archaic Greek poet Sappho.
The poem deals with the effects of ageing.
It is one of very few substantially complete poems by Sappho, and has been described as freer in its form than traditional Malay poetry.
Du Friedefürst, Herr Jesu Christ, BWV 116, is a church cantata written by Johann Sebastian Bach in 1724.
The cantata is based upon Jakob Ebert's hymn 'Du Friedefürst, Herr Jesu Christ'.
He led the first performance on 26 November 1724, concluding the liturgical year of 1724.
Nyanyi Sunyi is a 1937 poetry collection by Amir Hamzah.
Analysis of Nyanyi Sunyi has focused on the theme of God and His relationship to humanity.
The collection has been noted as difficult to read owing to its usage of rarely used Malay and Javanese terms.
Gleichwie der Regen und Schnee vom Himmel fällt, BWV 18, is an early church cantata by Johann Sebastian Bach.
Bach structured the work in five movements.
The text cites Isaiah, related to the gospel, the parable of the Sower.
Es ist das Heil uns kommen her, BWV 9, is a church cantata by Johann Sebastian Bach.
The theme is salvation from sin by God's grace alone.
The cantata is structured in seven movements, framed as the earlier chorale cantatas.

The impedance analogy is a method of representing a mechanical system by an analogous electrical system.
The mathematical behaviour of the simulated electrical system is identical to the mathematical behaviour of the represented mechanical system.
The impedance analogy is widely used to model the behaviour of mechanical filters.
Another very common use is in the field of audio equipment, such as loudspeakers.

Another very common use is in the field of audio equipment, such as loudspeakers.

Prototype filters are electronic filter designs that are used as a template to produce a modified filter design for a particular application.
